  • Don’t mutate the same source you are watching.
  • If you must, add guards (if conditions, equality checks).
  • Prefer deriving state with computed instead of syncing state with watchers.

6. Subtle watchEffect trap: accidental dependencies

Because watchEffect tracks everything you read, even a console.log(someRef.value) or a debug read can become a dependency and retrigger the effect.

7. Cleanup and async effects

Both APIs support cleanup via onCleanup, which is critical for cancelling requests or timers.

8. Practical rule of thumb

- If you can describe the dependency in one sentence: use watch().
- If the dependency is “whatever I touch in here”: use watchEffect().
- If you’re syncing state: ask yourself if this should be a computed instead.
Mental model: watch is declarative (“watch this”), watchEffect is reactive magic (“rerun when anything I use changes”). Magic is convenient — and that’s why it’s easier to shoot yourself in the foot.

Summary
  • watch() is explicit, lazy, and gives you old/new values.
  • watchEffect() is automatic, eager, and tracks dependencies implicitly.
  • Infinite loops happen when the effect mutates the same state it depends on.
  • Prefer computed for derived state, and use guards when mutating inside watchers.

seo.title: When should you use watch vs watchEffect in Vue?
seo.description: Use watch for explicit sources and watchEffect for automatic tracking. Infinite loops happen when an effect writes to the same reactive state it reads.

incidentCard.title: Effect Tracking Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate swaps a targeted `watch()` for `watchEffect()` in a data-fetching flow, then adds a few extra reactive reads inside the effect and accidentally creates noisy reruns. They say, "These APIs are basically interchangeable, so picking one is mostly style." Another teammate says, "Not really. `watch()` is explicit and predictable, while `watchEffect()` automatically tracks everything it touches and runs immediately. That convenience also makes it easier to create accidental dependencies or self-triggering loops." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. `watch()` is best when you know the exact source you care about, while `watchEffect()` is useful for broad automatic tracking but easier to over-trigger or loop if it reads and writes the wrong reactive state.
The first explanation is better. Both APIs react to the same changes in the same way, so the difference is mostly naming preference rather than dependency behavior or loop risk.
The better explanation is that `watchEffect()` is usually the safer default because Vue automatically filters out accidental dependencies and prevents self-triggered loops for you.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key lesson is that `watch()` and `watchEffect()` behave differently. `watch()` is explicit and predictable, while `watchEffect()` eagerly tracks whatever reactive values it touches, which is convenient but easier to misuse.

title: What does nextTick() do in Vue, and why are DOM measurements wrong immediately after a state change?
description: Explain what nextTick() does in Vue, how Vue batches and schedules DOM updates asynchronously, why the DOM is not updated immediately after reactive state changes, and when you must wait for nextTick() to safely read layout or measure elements.

blocks.text:
Overview

In Vue, state changes do not update the DOM synchronously. Vue batches multiple reactive updates and applies them in the next render cycle for performance. nextTick() lets you wait until Vue has flushed all pending DOM updates. If you measure the DOM immediately after changing state, you often read the old layout.

1. The core idea: Vue updates are async and batched

When you change reactive state, Vue does not patch the DOM immediately. It queues the component for re-render and processes all changes together in the next microtask tick.

2. The classic bug: measuring too early

This is why DOM measurements right after a state change are often wrong:
The template has not been re-rendered yet, so the element is still in its previous state.

3. nextTick(): wait until the DOM is updated

nextTick() returns a Promise that resolves after Vue has finished flushing the DOM updates for the current batch.

4. What nextTick() actually means

It does not mean “wait one frame” or “wait some time”. It means: wait until Vue’s pending render queue is flushed.

rows: Vue marks components as dirty and queues an update
Virtual DOM re-renders and patches the real DOM
blocks.caption: The Vue update cycle simplified

blocks.text:
5. Why Vue does this (performance)

If Vue updated the DOM immediately on every state change, multiple changes in one tick would cause multiple layouts, reflows, and repaints. By batching them, Vue can update the DOM once per tick.
6. Common real-world cases where you need nextTick()

rows: You need to measure its size after it appears
The element does not exist in the DOM yet
Scrolling to a newly rendered item
The element is not in the DOM until after render
They expect the DOM to already be updated
blocks.caption: Typical nextTick() use cases

blocks.text:
7. nextTick vs setTimeout / requestAnimationFrame

nextTick() waits for Vue’s render flush, not for a timer or the next frame. It is more precise and faster than setTimeout(..., 0) hacks.

8. Subtle case: multiple state changes

Even if you change state multiple times synchronously, Vue will still only update the DOM once, and nextTick() waits for that single final result.
Mental model: state changes schedule a render, nextTick waits for that render to finish.

Summary
  • Vue batches DOM updates and applies them asynchronously.
  • Right after a state change, the DOM is still in the old state.
  • nextTick() waits until Vue has flushed the pending DOM updates.
  • Use it whenever you need to read layout, measure elements, or interact with DOM that depends on the new state.

seo.title: What does nextTick do in Vue?
seo.description: nextTick waits for Vue to flush pending DOM updates after reactive state changes. Use it before reading layout, refs, or element measurements.

incidentCard.title: DOM Flush Timing Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate opens a panel with reactive state and immediately measures its height in the next line of code. When the number is wrong, they say, "Vue updated too slowly, so we should just wrap the measurement in `setTimeout` and hope the DOM is ready by then." Another teammate says, "The real issue is timing. Vue batches DOM updates, so right after a state change the DOM can still reflect the old render. `nextTick()` is the correct way to wait for Vue’s update queue to flush before measuring." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The second explanation is better. `nextTick()` is for waiting until Vue has applied pending DOM updates. If you need to read layout, refs, or newly rendered elements after state changes, that is the right boundary to wait for.
The first explanation is better. `setTimeout(..., 0)` is the safest way to handle Vue DOM timing because it waits longer than `nextTick()` and avoids depending on Vue internals.

The better explanation is that DOM measurement should always be correct immediately after a reactive assignment, since JavaScript state updates are synchronous and Vue does not really defer render work.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The key lesson is that reactive state changes and DOM updates do not happen at the same moment. Vue batches renders, and `nextTick()` is how you wait for that render flush before reading or measuring the DOM.

title: Why are keys critical in v-for? What exactly breaks when you use array index as a key?
description: Explain why keys are required in v-for, how Vue uses them during virtual DOM diffing, and what subtle but serious bugs happen when you use array index as a key (state reuse, wrong DOM nodes, broken animations, and input mix-ups).

seo.title: Why should you not use array index as key in Vue v-for?
seo.description: Using array index as key can make Vue reuse the wrong DOM nodes after list inserts or reorders. Use stable unique ids to keep state and inputs aligned.

blocks.text:
Overview

Keys are how Vue identifies which virtual DOM node corresponds to which real DOM node across renders. If keys are stable and unique, Vue can correctly reuse, move, or destroy elements. If keys are wrong (or you use the array index), Vue will reuse the wrong DOM nodes, causing bugs like inputs swapping values, components keeping the wrong state, and animations glitching.

1. What Vue actually does when a list changes

When a list rendered with v-for changes, Vue does not recreate the whole DOM. It diffs the old list and the new list and tries to match items using their key.

rows: Vue moves the correct DOM nodes
Vue reuses DOM nodes for the wrong items
Component state stays with the right item
Component state gets attached to the wrong item
blocks.caption: How keys affect Vue’s diffing behavior

blocks.text:
2. The correct pattern

Always use a stable, unique ID that represents the identity of the item, not its position.

3. The tempting but dangerous pattern

Using the array index looks fine… until the list changes.
4. What exactly breaks when you use index as key?

columns: What actually happens with index key
rows: New DOM node is created at top
Vue reuses every existing DOM node and just shifts data
That item’s DOM and component state are destroyed
A different item’s DOM gets reused and keeps stale state
Items move with their DOM and state
DOM stays in place, only data changes → state mismatch
Each input keeps its own value
Values appear to jump to other rows
blocks.caption: Concrete bugs caused by index as key

blocks.text: 5. The classic horror demo: inputs swapping values
When you click the button, the text you typed appears to move to a different row. Vue reused DOM nodes by position, not by identity.

6. Why Vue can’t magically fix this

Vue has no way to know whether an item at index 0 is “the same logical item as before” or a different one. The key is the only identity signal you give to the diffing algorithm.

7. When is index as key actually safe?

Almost never — but technically OK if all of these are true:
  • The list is static (no insert/remove/reorder).
  • The list items have no internal state (no inputs, no components with state).
  • You will never filter or sort it.
In practice: for real apps, just don’t.

8. How this relates to performance

Good keys don’t just fix bugs — they also make updates faster because Vue can move nodes instead of destroying and recreating them.
Mental model: keys tell Vue “who is who”. If you lie about identity (using index), Vue will faithfully do the wrong thing — very efficiently.

Summary
  • Keys define the identity of list items for Vue’s diffing algorithm.
  • Using index as key ties identity to position, not to the actual item.
  • This causes state to stick to the wrong DOM nodes: broken inputs, wrong component state, glitchy animations.
  • Always use a stable, unique ID from the data itself.

incidentCard.title: List Identity Check
incidentCard.scenario: A teammate renders a sortable list with `:key="index"` because "the rows are already in order, so index is simpler." It looks fine at first, but after someone inserts a new row at the top, text typed into one input suddenly appears under a different person. One teammate says, "Vue's diffing is flaky when lists reorder." Another says, "The real bug is that index describes position, not identity. Vue is reusing the wrong DOM nodes and component state because the keys shift when the list changes." Which explanation is better?

options.label: The first explanation is better. `v-for` with index keys is still safe because Vue rerenders the full list on every update, so reused DOM nodes cannot really cause state or input mix-ups.
The better explanation is that index keys only matter for move animations. Inputs, local component state, and DOM reuse are unaffected as long as the array contents stay the same.

The second explanation is better. Keys tell Vue which item is which across renders. If you use the array index, identity changes whenever the list is inserted into, removed from, or reordered, so Vue can keep the wrong DOM node, input value, or component state attached to the wrong item.

incidentCard.rereadPrompt: Not quite. The core lesson is that Vue keys represent identity, not position. When you key by index, reorders and inserts make Vue reuse the wrong DOM nodes and component state, which is why inputs and row state can jump to the wrong item.
